criteria: TokenType enum defines exactly these categories: NUMBER, STRING, IDENTIFIER, KEYWORD, PLUS, MINUS, STAR, SLASH, ASSIGN, EQUAL_EQUAL, BANG_EQUAL, LESS, LESS_EQUAL, GREATER, GREATER_EQUAL, LPAREN, RPAREN, LBRACE, RBRACE, LBRACKET, RBRACKET, SEMICOLON, COMMA, EOF, ERROR — all using Python's enum.Enum or equivalent
Token dataclass (or equivalent) stores four fields: type (TokenType), lexeme (str, the exact source text), line (int, 1-based), column (int, 1-based)
Scanner.__init__ accepts a source string and initializes: source, start (int), current (int), line=1, column=1, token_start_line=1, token_start_column=1, tokens=[]
Scanner.is_at_end() returns True if and only if self.current >= len(self.source)
Scanner.advance() consumes and returns the current character, increments self.current, increments self.column for non-newline characters, and on '\n' increments self.line and resets self.column to 1
Scanner.peek() returns self.source[self.current] without incrementing self.current, and returns '\0' (or equivalent non-matching sentinel) when is_at_end() is True
Scanner.scan_tokens() returns a list[Token] that always ends with exactly one EOF token, even for empty input
Single-character tokens (+, -, *, /, (, ), { }, [ ], ;, ,) are each recognized and emitted as their corresponding TokenType with correct lexeme and start position
Whitespace characters (space, tab, '\r', '\n') are consumed by advance() and do not produce any token in the output list
Windows line endings (\r\n) do not double-count lines: only '\n' increments self.line; '\r' increments only self.column
Token start position (line, column) is snapshotted at the beginning of each token, so multi-character tokens report the position of their first character, not their last
Unrecognized/invalid characters (e.g., @, #, $) produce an ERROR token whose lexeme is the single unrecognized character and whose line/column reflect the character's position in source
Empty string input produces a token list of exactly [Token(EOF, '', 1, 1)]
Token.__repr__ (or __str__) displays type name, lexeme, line, and column in a readable format for debugging
The _match(expected) method conditionally consumes the next character only if it equals expected, using advance() internally (not direct index increment) to ensure position tracking remains correct
Two-character operator '==' is emitted as a single EQUAL_EQUAL token; the input '==' must produce exactly one token of type EQUAL_EQUAL, not two ASSIGN tokens
Single '=' not followed by '=' is emitted as ASSIGN; the input '=' must produce exactly one ASSIGN token
Two-character operator '!=' is emitted as a single BANG_EQUAL token; '!' not followed by '=' produces an ERROR token
'<' is emitted as LESS when not followed by '='; '<=' is emitted as LESS_EQUAL as a single token
'>' is emitted as GREATER when not followed by '='; '>=' is emitted as GREATER_EQUAL as a single token
Input '>==' tokenizes as exactly two tokens: GREATER_EQUAL (lexeme '>='  ) followed by ASSIGN (lexeme '='), demonstrating left-to-right maximal munch
Input '===' tokenizes as EQUAL_EQUAL followed by ASSIGN, not three separate tokens
Integer literals consisting of one or more digits (e.g., '0', '42', '100') are emitted as NUMBER tokens with the exact digit sequence as lexeme
Float literals with digits on both sides of a decimal point (e.g., '3.14', '0.5') are emitted as NUMBER tokens with the full float string as lexeme
Input '3.' tokenizes as NUMBER('3') followed by ERROR('.'); the trailing dot is not consumed as part of the number
Input '.5' produces an ERROR token for '.' followed by NUMBER('5'); leading-dot floats are not supported
Input '3.foo' produces NUMBER('3'), ERROR('.'), IDENTIFIER('foo') — two-character peek prevents consuming '.' as part of float when followed by non-digit
_peek_next() returns the character at index current+1 without consuming, returning '\0' when current+1 is out of bounds
Identifiers start with a letter (a-z, A-Z) or underscore, followed by zero or more letters, digits, or underscores; all such sequences produce IDENTIFIER tokens
Identifier scanning uses _current_lexeme() only after the while loop has consumed all valid identifier characters, ensuring no premature keyword matching
All seven keywords (if, else, while, return, true, false, null) are recognized and emitted as KEYWORD tokens via post-scan dictionary lookup
An identifier that begins with a keyword prefix (e.g., 'iffy', 'returning', 'nullify') is emitted as a single IDENTIFIER token, not split into keyword + remainder
The keyword table is consulted only after the complete identifier lexeme is accumulated — keyword recognition is a dictionary lookup, not character-level pattern matching
The characters '=', '!', '<', '>' are removed from SINGLE_CHAR_TOKENS and handled exclusively by the two-character operator logic to prevent incorrect single-character emission

Input 'if (x >= 42) { return true; }' produces exactly the token stream: KEYWORD('if'), LPAREN, IDENTIFIER('x'), GREATER_EQUAL('>='), NUMBER('42'), RPAREN, LBRACE, KEYWORD('return'), KEYWORD('true'), SEMICOLON, RBRACE, EOF
Input '42abc' produces NUMBER('42') followed by IDENTIFIER('abc') — number scanning stops at the first non-digit, non-dot character
All token positions (line, column) for multi-character tokens reflect the position of the token's first character, not its last
String literals delimited by double quotes are scanned and emitted as STRING tokens whose lexeme includes the opening and closing quote characters and all raw source text between them
Escape sequences \n, \t, \r, \", \\ inside string literals are consumed correctly as two-character sequences without terminating the string scan
A string containing a comment delimiter ('// ...' or '/* ... */') is emitted as a single STRING token — the comment characters have no special meaning inside string mode
An unterminated string that reaches EOF without a closing quote emits exactly one ERROR token whose line and column match the position of the opening double-quote character

An unterminated string terminated by a newline (\n inside the string before a closing quote) emits exactly one ERROR token at the position of the opening quote, and scanning continues on the next line
A backslash at the end of input inside a string (no escape character following it) emits an ERROR token for the unterminated string

An unknown escape sequence (e.g., \q, \x) inside a string emits an ERROR token for the two-character bad escape and continues scanning for the closing quote — the string token may still be emitted if the closing quote is found
Single-line comments starting with '//' consume all characters until end of line without emitting any token, and do NOT consume the terminating newline character
The division operator '/' followed by a non-'/' and non-'*' character is correctly emitted as a SLASH token and is not confused with a comment delimiter
Block comments delimited by '/*' and '*/' consume all characters between the delimiters including newlines without emitting any token
A single asterisk inside a block comment that is not immediately followed by '/' does not close the comment — scanning continues
Block comments do NOT nest: '/* /* */ ...' closes at the first '*/' encountered, and subsequent text is scanned in NORMAL mode
An unterminated block comment (EOF reached before '*/') emits exactly one ERROR token whose line and column match the position of the opening '/*'
Line numbers are correctly incremented for every '\n' consumed inside a block comment, so that the first token after the comment has an accurate line number
Line numbers and column numbers of tokens after block comments and string literals are correct when those constructs contain embedded newlines

The '/' character is dispatched correctly: '//' enters line comment mode, '/*' enters block comment mode, and '/' alone emits SLASH — all three cases handled in a single branch using _match() lookahead
The canonical token stream test for 'if (x >= 42) { return true; }' asserts all 11 non-EOF tokens token-by-token, verifying type, lexeme, line, and column for each token
scan_tokens() always returns a list ending with exactly one EOF token, verified across empty input, valid input, and input containing errors

After encountering an unrecognized character (e.g. '@'), the scanner emits one ERROR token with the correct lexeme and position, then continues scanning and correctly emits all subsequent valid tokens
Multiple invalid characters in a single input (e.g. '@#$') each produce a separate ERROR token, with no errors silently swallowed and no valid tokens lost between them
An unterminated string literal on line N does not prevent valid tokens from being emitted on line N+1 — the scanner recovers and continues after the error
An unterminated block comment produces exactly one ERROR token at the position of the opening '/*', and code appearing inside the unterminated comment is not tokenized
Empty string input produces exactly one token: EOF at line=1, column=1
A single valid character (e.g. '+') produces the correct token type followed by EOF
A single invalid character (e.g. '@') produces ERROR followed by EOF
An identifier of 1,000 characters is scanned as a single IDENTIFIER token with a lexeme of length 1,000
A number of 500 digits is scanned as a single NUMBER token
A string literal with 10,000 characters of content is scanned as a single STRING token
All single-character tokens scanned consecutively with no whitespace separators (e.g. '+-*/(){}[];,') are correctly split into individual tokens
Keywords appearing at the very end of input with no trailing whitespace or newline are emitted as KEYWORD tokens (not lost due to missing delimiter)

Token line numbers are correct for a complete multi-line program: tokens after a single-line comment appear on the next line, tokens after a multi-line block comment appear on the correct post-comment line
Column numbers reset to 1 on every newline character, verified across multiple lines

line endings increment the line counter exactly once (not twice), so tokens after 
 have the same line number as they would after 
 alone
Tab characters advance the column counter by exactly 1 (not by a tab-stop width)
Position tracking does not drift over 50 consecutive lines: a token on line 50 is reported at line=50, column=1
EOF token has the correct line and column (the position immediately after the last character consumed)
Tokenizing a 10,000-line input completes in under 1.0 second measured with time.perf_counter()
Scanner throughput exceeds 100,000 characters per second on the 10,000-line benchmark
The generated 10,000-line benchmark input contains no ERROR tokens, confirming the source is valid and the benchmark measures scanning not error handling
scan_tokens() called on two independent Scanner instances with identical source produces identical token streams (type, lexeme, line, column) for every token

TokenType enumeration defines all categories: NUMBER, STRING, IDENTIFIER, KEYWORD, PLUS, MINUS, STAR, SLASH, ASSIGN, EQUAL_EQUAL, BANG_EQUAL, LESS, LESS_EQUAL, GREATER, GREATER_EQUAL, LPAREN, RPAREN, LBRACE, RBRACE, LBRACKET, RBRACKET, SEMICOLON, COMMA, EOF, ERROR
Token dataclass stores: type (TokenType), lexeme (str), line (int, 1-based), column (int, 1-based)
Token equality compares all four fields; two Token instances with identical fields are equal
Scanner.advance() consumes and returns the current character and is the sole updater of self.line and self.column
Scanner.advance() increments self.line and resets self.column to 1 when ch == '\n'
Scanner.advance() increments self.column by 1 for all non-newline characters including '\r' and '\t'
Scanner.peek() returns the next character without consuming it; self.current, self.line, self.column are unchanged after peek()
Scanner.peek() returns '\0' (null character) when is_at_end() is True
Scanner.is_at_end() returns True when self.current >= len(self.source)
Scanner tracks token_start_line and token_start_column by calling _begin_token() before consuming each token's first character
Token.line and Token.column reflect the position of the token's first character, not the position after the last character
Single-character tokens (+, -, *, /, (, ), { }, [ ], ;, ,, =) are each recognized and emitted as the correct TokenType
Whitespace characters (space, tab, \r, \n) are consumed without emitting any token
Windows line endings (\r\n) are treated as a single newline: line increments once, not twice
EOF token is emitted as the last token with lexeme='' and position reflecting the cursor after all source is consumed
Empty input ('') produces exactly one token: EOF with line=1, column=1
Unrecognized characters produce an ERROR token with the bad character as lexeme and the correct line/column position
Scanning continues after an ERROR token; subsequent valid tokens are still emitted
Multiple unrecognized characters each produce separate ERROR tokens; all are collected in the token list
scan_tokens() returns a list (not a generator); the list always ends with exactly one EOF token
Two-character operators == != <= >= are recognized as single tokens (not two separate tokens)
Maximal munch: '==' is emitted as EQUAL_EQUAL, never as ASSIGN + ASSIGN
Single '=' is emitted as ASSIGN only when not followed by '='
'<' is emitted as LESS; '<=' is emitted as LESS_EQUAL (maximal munch applied)
Integer literals: sequences of digits (e.g., '42', '0') are scanned as NUMBER tokens
Float literals: digit sequences with one decimal point (e.g., '3.14') are scanned as NUMBER tokens
Identifiers: sequences starting with letter or underscore, followed by alphanumeric or underscore
Keywords (if, else, while, return, true, false, null) are identified by lookup table after scanning identifier
Keywords are emitted as KEYWORD tokens not IDENTIFIER tokens
Input '>== ' is tokenized as GREATER_EQUAL + ASSIGN (maximal munch: '>=' then '=')
String literals between double quotes are scanned as STRING tokens with lexeme including surrounding quotes
Escape sequences \n, \t, \r, \", \\ are recognized as valid within strings; scanner continues after them
Unterminated string (EOF before closing quote) produces ERROR token at the opening quote position
Unterminated string (newline before closing quote) produces ERROR token at the opening quote position
Backslash as final character in source inside a string produces ERROR token
Unknown escape sequence (e.g., \q) produces ERROR token and scanning continues to find closing quote (error recovery)
Unknown escape followed by closing quote produces both ERROR and STRING tokens in the stream
Single-line comments (//) skip all characters until end of line and emit no tokens
Single-line comment does NOT consume the trailing newline — newline is left for main loop
Multi-line block comments (/* */) skip all content including newlines and emit no tokens
Unterminated block comment (no closing */) produces ERROR token at the position of the opening /
Multi-line block comments do NOT nest: /* /* */ closes at the first */ encountered
Comments inside strings are NOT treated as comments: "hello // world" is a single STRING token
Strings inside comments are NOT treated as strings: // "hello" produces no tokens
Line numbers are correctly updated inside multi-line block comments via advance()
Line numbers are correctly updated after a newline-terminated string
Token after a multi-line block comment reports the correct line number
Division operator / is still emitted as SLASH when not followed by / or *
/ removed from SINGLE_CHAR_TOKENS; all / handling goes through explicit dispatch branch
VALID_ESCAPE_CHARS constant defined at module level as frozenset containing exactly n, t, r, ", \
All M1 and M2 tests continue to pass after M3 changes (regression-free)
scan_tokens() always ends with exactly one EOF token after M3 changes
_scan_line_comment() is O(k) in comment length with no token emission
_scan_block_comment() is O(k) with no backtracking; uses peek-then-advance pattern for */ detection
_scan_string() is O(k) single pass; no character-by-character string concatenation
Complete multi-line test program is tokenized into correct token stream (verified token-by-token)
Error recovery: after encountering an invalid character, scanning continues from the next character
Multiple errors in a single input are all reported (not just the first)
Token stream for 'if (x >= 42) { return true; }' produces exactly: Keyword(if), LParen, Ident(x), GreaterEqual, Number(42), RParen, LBrace, Keyword(return), Keyword(true), Semicolon, RBrace, EOF
Edge cases tested: empty input (produces only EOF), single character input, maximum-length identifiers
Performance: tokenizing a 10,000-line input completes in under 1 second
All token positions (line, column) are accurate for a multi-line test file
